chapters.title: Spiritual Materialism
chapters.description: Using Trungpa’s language, Matthew describes how even awakening can be taken up by ego, turning practice into striving, clinging, and self-concern.

chapters.title: What Insight Does Not Buy
chapters.description: He reflects on the limits of insight experiences, including the painful fact that deep realization can still coexist with harmful behavior.

chapters.title: Emptiness And Longing
chapters.description: The talk shifts to the cultivation side, with Leonard Cohen’s poem and an exploration of dissatisfaction, longing, and why ordinary pleasure does not fully quiet the heart.

chapters.title: Freedom As Experience
chapters.description: Matthew cites Nisargadatta, then reframes the passage as a phenomenological description of freedom rather than a metaphysical claim.

chapters.title: Harmlessness And Service
chapters.description: In Q&A, he says the two paths converge in harmlessness, which includes not only refraining from harm but also a life of service, giving, and love.

title: Practice Note
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: What happens when life stops feeling like a chain of projects? The talk looks at the stress that comes from turning tasks, meditation, walking, even going to the bathroom or making tea into something to finish, and invites a gentler experiment: do what needs doing without fixing each moment into a goal. The emphasis is on relaxing that project-making mind and staying open, moment by moment, to what is here and what is skillful.
short_summary: A brief practice note on loosening the habit of turning everyday life and meditation into projects.

chapters.title: Life as a Project
chapters.description: He introduces the tendency to turn life into tasks and explains why that creates strain.

chapters.title: Not Doing Nothing
chapters.description: He clarifies that releasing project-thinking does not mean becoming passive or avoiding responsibility.

chapters.title: Meditation as Task
chapters.description: He notes that even walking meditation can become another project, and invites a softer way of practicing.

chapters.title: Relaxing the Schedule
chapters.description: He describes how the mind organizes practice by time, steps, and outcomes, and what changes when that grip loosens.

chapters.title: Ordinary Actions
chapters.description: He brings the point into daily life with examples like walking, using the bathroom, and having tea.

chapters.title: Open to What Is Skillful
chapters.description: He closes by inviting a whole-morning attitude of openness to the present moment and what is skillful.

title: Dharmette
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: What is mindfulness, really: a loose umbrella word, or a set of capacities that can actually be trained? The talk begins by noticing how mindfulness is used in modern Buddhist and secular settings, then turns to a working definition drawn from Shinzen Young: concentration, clarity, and equanimity. From there it moves through each quality in turn, showing how gathered attention, higher-resolution seeing, and not pushing or pulling on experience can change what practice feels like from the inside. The talk closes with a simple self-check: look at where these three qualities are strong or weak and use that to orient practice more skillfully.
short_summary: A short exploration of mindfulness as concentration, clarity, and equanimity, with attention to how those three qualities change practice.

chapters.title: What mindfulness means
chapters.description: Matthew opens by saying mindfulness is both the beginning and the fruition of the path, and that it is worth clarifying even if it cannot be pinned down perfectly.

chapters.title: Mindfulness in the mainstream
chapters.description: He draws on Jon Kabat-Zinn’s 2011 framing to show how mindfulness has become a broad umbrella term and why that can be both useful and confusing.

chapters.title: A threefold model
chapters.description: Matthew introduces Shinzen Young’s nontraditional synthesis of mindfulness as a working definition organized around three skills.

chapters.title: Gathered attention
chapters.description: He describes concentration as the ability to place attention where you want it, and explains how a stable mind changes the flavor of practice and daily life.

chapters.title: Seeing clearly
chapters.description: The talk shifts to clarity as higher resolution perception, where experience is seen in detail rather than collapsed into solid things and self.

chapters.title: Not pushing or pulling
chapters.description: Using the Bahiya Sutta, Matthew explains equanimity as allowing experience to arise and pass without grasping or resistance.

chapters.title: Checking practice
chapters.description: He closes by suggesting a practical self-inquiry into concentration, clarity, and equanimity, encouraging listeners to take what is useful and leave the rest.

title: Mudita
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: What happens when the heart stops treating another person’s good fortune as a threat? The talk moves from the ordinary claim that mindfulness gives us more choice into a close look at mudita, sympathetic joy, as a habit the mind can actually learn over time. Through a Maurice Sendak story, a passage from Sam Harris, and reflections on envy and jealousy, the speaker shows how comparison narrows the heart and how letting go of self-protection makes room for delight in others’ well-being. The talk ends with a quiet return to practice, suggesting that joy becomes steadier as the sense of territory loosens.
short_summary: A talk on mudita that traces sympathetic joy from inspiring examples through the psychology of envy, self-clinging, and the gradual opening of the heart.

chapters.title: Practice and Momentum
chapters.description: Brensilver frames Dharma practice as a gradual reshaping of the mind, where repeated wholesome moments build momentum and change what the mind defaults to.

chapters.title: What Mudita Means
chapters.description: He introduces mudita as open-hearted delight in another's well-being, without a sense of encroachment or defense.

chapters.title: Sendak’s Eaten Card
chapters.description: A Maurice Sendak story illustrates sympathetic joy as delight so open that the gift is received without possessiveness.

chapters.title: Love Broadens Out
chapters.description: A passage from Sam Harris is used to show how love for one friend can open into a more universal and less territorial love.

chapters.title: Joy Can Be Trained
chapters.description: Brensilver turns to a canonical Buddhist statement and explains that mudita is cultivated by removing obstacles rather than forcing a smile.

chapters.title: Envy and Comparison
chapters.description: He distinguishes envy and jealousy, then traces how comparison and scarcity make another person's happiness feel threatening.

chapters.title: Evolution and Letting Go
chapters.description: A David Buss passage about jealousy's evolutionary logic helps him contrast biological fitness with the Buddhist path of letting go.

chapters.title: Self-View as Obstacle
chapters.description: Through examples and quotations from Zigar Kangtrul Rinpoche and Mathieu Ricard, he shows how envy exposes the wounds of ego and self-importance.

chapters.title: Less Territorial, More Free
chapters.description: The talk closes by linking insight into the First Noble Truth with a lighter, less possessive heart, ending in a short sit and a blessing for shared goodness.

title: Some Fruits of Practice
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: The talk begins with a simple question: what is a Dharma talk for? From there it moves through three functions — instruction, gladdening the mind, and support — and uses that frame to explore how practice gradually softens doubt, aligns conduct with intention, and makes life feel less fragmented. The later sections turn toward the fruits of practice: less self-clinging, more openness, a quieter heart, and a growing sense that everything in life can belong on the path.
short_summary: A reflection on how practice changes over time: from instruction and encouragement to greater ease, less self-clinging, and a life that feels more fully on the path.

chapters.title: Three functions of talk
chapters.description: Matthew opens with the Buddha's framing of Dharma talks as instructional, gladdening, and supportive, and explains why he wants this talk to emphasize support.

chapters.title: Why fruits matter
chapters.description: He talks about how practitioners often feel they are doing practice wrong, and why reflecting on the fruits can steady and encourage the heart.

chapters.title: Ethics becomes natural
chapters.description: The talk turns to how behavior can come into closer alignment with intention, and how sila can gradually feel less like sacrifice and more like ease.

chapters.title: Wholeness and trust
chapters.description: Matthew describes practice as digesting old suffering, reducing doubt, and making one's whole life feel like the path.

chapters.title: Path becomes personal
chapters.description: He explores how practice can feel deeply one's own, bringing safety, responsibility, and a quieter question about what really matters.

chapters.title: Wanting less
chapters.description: Using affective forecasting and resilience, he suggests that practice can make us more realistic about happiness, simpler in desire, and less burdened by wanting.

chapters.title: Self-clinging loosens
chapters.description: The talk moves into the burden of self-referential thinking, illustrated by Ajahn Sumedho and Ajahn Amaro, and distinguishes self-care from self-clinging.

chapters.title: The vase and the heart
chapters.description: Through the image of carrying a fragile vase, Matthew shows how protecting a phantom self constricts life, while letting that burden down makes life feel safer and more open.

chapters.title: Wisdom and love
chapters.description: He closes by saying that seeing clearly and loving deeply belong together, then uses a story about a tiny turn of attention becoming a life to encourage staying present.

title: Practice Questions
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: How do you practice when desire is pulling in different directions—when goals matter, but the mind keeps turning them into a promise of happiness? The talk moves through a series of questions about wholesome desire and craving, why success can still leave us unsettled, and how taking care of this moment is a more reliable way of caring for the future. It then turns practical: how to keep practice alive in a busy life, how much solitude or contemplation is enough, and whether joy has a place in the path before the hard work feels complete.
short_summary: A question-and-answer talk on goals, practice, and the difference between craving and wholesome aspiration, ending with a clear case for present-moment care and the quiet emergence of joy.

chapters.title: Opening Questions
chapters.description: Matthew invites questions about practice and integration of Dharma into daily life.

chapters.title: Goals and Craving
chapters.description: He distinguishes wholesome desire from craving and explains why practice asks us to watch how we hold goals.

chapters.title: Success and Letdown
chapters.description: Using Adam Phillips and examples of accomplishment, he points to the gap between success and inner fulfillment.

chapters.title: Take Care of This Moment
chapters.description: He brings in Thich Nhat Hanh and argues that caring for the future happens through present-moment attention.

chapters.title: Busy Life Practice
chapters.description: In response to a question about limited time, he suggests retreat planning, daily contact with the cushion, and keeping a thread to practice even in short sittings.

chapters.title: Solitude and Contemplation
chapters.description: He addresses the need for quiet and reflection, while warning against using solitude to cut off from life or relationship.

chapters.title: Dharma in Daily Life
chapters.description: The conversation turns to how reading, reflection, and lived practice deepen one another as the Dharma comes to shape the whole of life.

chapters.title: Practice and Joy
chapters.description: Matthew closes by saying that the path talks about suffering because dismantling suffering leaves goodness, relief, and a more natural joy.

title: Knowing and Not Knowing
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: How does practice deepen both knowing and not knowing? The talk moves from mindfulness as a way of seeing experience more clearly—breath, body, pleasure, pain, habits, and the ordinary facts of being human—into a quieter argument that fixed views can block insight and intimacy. Along the way, it uses Rilke, Joseph Goldstein, a Zen tea story, and reflections on science and relationships to show why openness, wonder, and the willingness to be wrong matter on the path.
short_summary: A talk on mindfulness as clearer knowing and the equally necessary practice of not clinging to certainty.

chapters.title: Knowing the path
chapters.description: Matthew opens by framing the Buddhist path as a way of knowing more deeply rather than remaining lost in vague experience.

chapters.title: Seeing through experience
chapters.description: He unpacks vipassana as seeing through the strands of experience and noticing the breath, body, feeling tones, and mind states more clearly.

chapters.title: Bare knowledge
chapters.description: Using Joseph Goldstein, he emphasizes the plainness of present-moment awareness and how easily we overlook it while chasing something special.

chapters.title: Self-knowledge and habit
chapters.description: He turns to the difficulty of knowing ourselves, naming habit, ego-protection, and the mismatch between our self-story and what is actually there.

chapters.title: What helps well-being
chapters.description: Matthew brings in Erica Carlson and related research, then asks what really makes us happy and why inherited models of success often fail.

chapters.title: False refuges
chapters.description: He names wealth, power, and fame as unstable refuges and ties the discussion back to the Four Noble Truths and a heartfelt path.

chapters.title: The value of not knowing
chapters.description: A Zen tea story and Stuart Firestein's writing on ignorance shift the talk toward wonder, openness, and the need to loosen attachment to views.

chapters.title: Views and practice
chapters.description: He describes how craving, aversion, and unconsciousness shape our theories, then argues that practice is an encounter with mystery that cannot be fully controlled or known in advance.

chapters.title: Relationships and learning
chapters.description: Philip Roth and Stephen Mitchell help him show why we never fully know another person, and he ends by saying meditation is ongoing learning rather than arrival.

title: In The Gap
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: The talk turns to the quiet moments between formal practice: the walk to the bathroom, the shift out of sitting, the places where habit quietly takes over. Rather than pushing for constant vigilance, it asks what the minimum useful effort of mindfulness might be, and suggests that the urge for unconsciousness often comes from trying too hard. With just a little less strain, what feels spacey can sometimes open into real spaciousness.
short_summary: A brief talk about noticing the gaps between mindful moments and finding the minimum effort that lets spaciness soften into spaciousness.

chapters.title: Noticing the gaps
chapters.description: The talk begins by pointing to the gaps and transitions where attention often drops away and habits reveal themselves.

chapters.title: Ordinary transitions
chapters.description: Brensilver describes how the mind defaults in small movements like getting up and walking away from practice.

chapters.title: Habit hides there
chapters.description: He emphasizes that suffering and habitual patterns often appear most clearly in the in-between moments.

chapters.title: The cost of strain
chapters.description: The talk turns to the feeling that continuous mindfulness can be tiring and that the mind may want unconsciousness as relief.

chapters.title: Less effort needed
chapters.description: He reframes mindfulness as using only the minimum effort required, without adding unnecessary strain.

chapters.title: From spaciness to space
chapters.description: The talk closes by suggesting that a small shift in effort can transform spaciness into a sense of spaciousness.

title: Failing Well
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: What if failure is not a detour from the path, but one of the ways the path works? The talk begins with perfectionism as a familiar form of self-harshness and constant checking, then turns toward a different stance: start, continue, and let the practice include not getting it right. A story about a mistaken gift and the response, “It’s such a good mistake,” opens into a gentler view of learning, where metta and repeated beginning again soften the heart.
short_summary: A brief talk on perfectionism, failure, and how learning to fail well can become part of meditation practice.

chapters.title: Perfectionism and the path
chapters.description: Brensilver introduces the tension between lofty spiritual aspirations and the perfectionistic mind that wants to avoid disappointment.

chapters.title: How it shows up
chapters.description: He names the familiar forms perfectionism takes in ordinary life and meditation: harshness, striving, and relentless self-checking.

chapters.title: Striving and comparison
chapters.description: The talk turns to how spiritual practice can become a site of benchmarking, comparison, and spiritual materialism.

chapters.title: Connection instead of separation
chapters.description: He reframes the path as one of deeper connection and suggests that perfectionism tends to isolate us from ourselves and others.

chapters.title: Failing as practice
chapters.description: Drawing on Norman Fischer and Beckett, he argues that ongoing practice depends on the willingness to fail, begin again, and resist the demand for success.

chapters.title: The good mistake
chapters.description: A retreat story about a misplaced gift becomes an example of how generosity can hold error without self-condemnation.

chapters.title: Metta and innocence
chapters.description: Brensilver closes by linking failure with metta, tenderness toward the First Noble Truth, and a more innocent way of meeting life.

title: Secrets, Shame and Transparency
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: What happens when secrecy is treated not just as a private problem, but as a place where the practice has not yet reached? The talk begins with a contrast between depth and breadth in practice: the mind can settle more deeply, but the Dharma can also spread into the hidden corners of life until less and less feels protected from awareness. From there, it turns to shame, honesty, and the stress of keeping secrets, using examples from Foucault, Sam Harris, and the Buddha’s teaching on conscience and concern for consequences to ask how transparency becomes a form of safety for oneself and others. A final exchange with the audience brings the question into focus by teasing apart authenticity and privacy, and asking what it means to live with less to hide without losing good boundaries.
short_summary: The talk explores how secrecy, shame, honesty, and privacy shape practice, and argues that growing transparency can make us safer, kinder, and more connected.

chapters.title: Depth and breadth
chapters.description: He begins by describing practice as moving in two directions at once: deeper realization and a wider reach into ordinary life.

chapters.title: Becoming transparent
chapters.description: He develops the idea that Dharma can spread into more corners of experience, softening hidden places and making us safer for others.

chapters.title: Turning to secrets
chapters.description: He shifts from general practice to the subject of secrets, naming the stress of self-protection and the pull toward freedom and transparency.

chapters.title: Privacy and shame
chapters.description: He distinguishes secrecy from privacy and explores how moralism and shame can lead us to hide even from ourselves.

chapters.title: Honesty as a mirror
chapters.description: Using Sam Harris and a few playful examples, he argues that honesty reveals how we relate to reality and to one another.

chapters.title: Suppressing the hidden
chapters.description: Drawing on Daniel Wegner, he shows how trying to suppress thoughts or secrets tends to intensify preoccupation and anxiety.

chapters.title: Wholesome shame
chapters.description: He turns to the Buddha’s teaching on hiri and ottappa, distinguishing conscience from toxic shame and linking ethical discomfort to practice and behavioral change.

chapters.title: Kindness and vulnerability
chapters.description: He looks at the unproductive forms of shame, then emphasizes kindness, sangha, and compassion as ways to breathe around vulnerable places in the mind.

chapters.title: Questions on authenticity
chapters.description: In dialogue with the audience, he clarifies the relation between authenticity, self-inquiry, privacy, and transparency, ending with a short metta-style close.

title: Practice Note
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: The talk begins with a practical question: how should concentration and mindfulness be understood in relation to each other? Rather than treating them as separate, the speaker reframes them as deeply intertwined, arguing that mindfulness without concentration can become scattered, while concentration gives mindfulness its gathered, rich quality. The discussion moves from a sutta-based reference to a more technical definition that folds concentration into mindfulness itself, then circles back to the listener’s experience of “ping-pong” attention and how practice can become more saturated and intimate with each object.
short_summary: A short practice discussion on why mindfulness and concentration are not easily separated, and how gathered attention makes mindfulness feel more alive.

chapters.title: Opening Question
chapters.description: Matthew invites questions and briefly acknowledges the session’s format before turning to the practice issue at hand.

chapters.title: Mindfulness or Concentration
chapters.description: A listener asks how to understand the relative importance of concentration and mindfulness in practice.

chapters.title: A Continuum Model
chapters.description: Matthew describes one common way of seeing the two as broad and narrow attention, then says he prefers a different framing.

chapters.title: Gathered Attention
chapters.description: He explains that mindfulness without concentration can feel scattered, while rich mindfulness involves awareness being poured into the object.

chapters.title: Definitions And Insight
chapters.description: Matthew cites Thanissaro Bhikkhu and a Shinzen Young lineage teacher to show how mindfulness is often defined with concentration already included.

chapters.title: Concentration And Wisdom
chapters.description: He addresses the traditional idea that deep absorption lacks insight, while suggesting concentration still propels the path and is not fully separate from wisdom.

chapters.title: Saturated Awareness
chapters.description: In the closing exchange, Matthew describes a practice where attention moves among sensations and thoughts, yet each contact feels deeply tasted.

title: Reflection on Mortality: The Poignancy of Being Alive
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: A cemetery by the speaker’s workplace becomes a starting point for asking what it means to face mortality without flinching. The talk moves from personal reflections and the limits of “year to live” exercises into Buddhist teachings on impermanence, not-self, and the possibility that seeing death clearly can soften the heart rather than harden it. Along the way, the speaker weaves in Freud, Larry Rosenberg, Pema Chödrön, Mary Oliver, and a closing poem by Nyogen Senzaki to trace a path from denial toward a quieter, more open way of living.
short_summary: A reflection on mortality that moves from fear and denial toward impermanence, not-self, and the possibility of a more open heart.

chapters.title: A Cemetery Reminder
chapters.description: Matthew begins with a small but vivid encounter near a veterans cemetery and uses it to open the theme of mortality.

chapters.title: Mindfulness of Death
chapters.description: He invites listeners to notice their reactions to the topic and introduces mortality as something to be included in practice, not avoided.

chapters.title: Practicing the Last Year
chapters.description: He describes Stephen Levine’s year-to-live practice and a conversation with a Holocaust survivor that brings humility to the exercise.

chapters.title: Denial and Humility
chapters.description: Matthew reflects on why mortality feels distant even when it is always near, weaving in Freud and his own lineage of sensitivity around death.

chapters.title: Walking With Death
chapters.description: He cites Larry Rosenberg and then turns to the idea that death is not at the end of life but present throughout it.

chapters.title: Mortality Salience
chapters.description: Drawing on Terror Management Theory, he looks at how fear of death can harden identity, prejudice, and defensive behavior.

chapters.title: Kindness Under Groundlessness
chapters.description: Using Pema Chödrön and Mary Oliver, he shows how shared fragility can soften the heart and clarify what matters.

chapters.title: Safety in Change
chapters.description: Matthew returns to the Buddha’s search for safety amid impermanence and connects it to meditation as a practice of letting go.

chapters.title: The Phantom Self
chapters.description: He closes by linking death fear to self-view, then softens the talk with a poem by Nyogen Senzaki and a final blessing for open-hearted living.

title: Practice Questions
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: How do you keep practice steady when life is crowded, and how do you tell the difference between true meditation and the mind simply spinning? The talk moves from a practical question about fitting in practice with two young children to a set of clear suggestions: make a time and place, clarify the intention, and let meditation become as ordinary and non-negotiable as brushing your teeth. It then turns to the structure of practice itself, distinguishing breath- or body-based focus, open awareness, and times when thought is intentionally taken as the object of reflection rather than a distraction.
short_summary: A practical talk on making meditation regular and distinguishing focused practice from ordinary thinking or reflective contemplation.

chapters.title: Opening the question
chapters.description: Matthew invites questions about practice and hears a concern about inconsistency and how the mind should relate to thought.

chapters.title: Two parts of the concern
chapters.description: He names the two issues clearly: establishing regular practice and distinguishing meditation from ordinary thinking.

chapters.title: Make practice automatic
chapters.description: He offers practical advice about setting a time and place, reducing decision-making, and letting meditation become a settled habit like brushing teeth.

chapters.title: Intention and mortality
chapters.description: He points to sangha, renewed intention, and reflection on life’s finitude as deeper supports for returning to practice.

chapters.title: Clear instructions matter
chapters.description: Matthew explains that a vague sit invites the mind’s habitual tendencies, while a precise object such as breath, body, or sound helps define practice.

chapters.title: Noting and distraction
chapters.description: He describes noting as a way to mark focus and distraction clearly, and then distinguishes that from actively reflecting on a theme.

chapters.title: Thought as object
chapters.description: He contrasts breath practice, where thought is distraction, with open awareness or reflective practice in which thoughts can be included without solving anything.

chapters.title: Listener’s response
chapters.description: The questioner names what was helpful, especially the reminder to start simply and stay with one object rather than jumping around.

title: Anxiety
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Anxiety here is treated less as a private flaw than as a mix of useful alarm, restless thinking, and an uneasy hope that worrying will somehow keep life under control. The talk moves from fear on the edge of a cliff, to the joke of a 3 a.m. new worry, to psychological research on how worry stays trapped in future scenarios and overestimates what it can predict. From there, it turns toward practice: loosening the grip on control, working with uncertainty, untangling thought from body sensations, and learning that safety comes more from relinquishing than from finding the perfect path.
short_summary: A grounded talk on anxiety that traces worry from useful fear to the mind’s attempt to control the future, then points toward ease through uncertainty, honesty, and letting go.

chapters.title: What Anxiety Feels Like
chapters.description: He begins with a close look at the felt sense of anxiety as restless, changing thought layered over a deeper hum of worry.

chapters.title: Fear and Alarm
chapters.description: Matthew distinguishes fear from anxiety, using an Ajahn Chah example to show fear as a useful response to immediate danger.

chapters.title: Worry as a Habit
chapters.description: A short Onion parody introduces the strange productivity of worry and sets up the psychological discussion that follows.

chapters.title: Future-Making Mind
chapters.description: Drawing on Thomas Borkovec, he explains worry as self-talk aimed at preventing imagined harm and notes how it can keep life lived in illusion.

chapters.title: Forks in the Road
chapters.description: He questions the fantasy that one decisive choice will solve everything and reframes the first noble truth as a more honest relationship to uncertainty.

chapters.title: Groundlessness and Practice
chapters.description: Using Joseph Goldstein and mindfulness research, he points toward learning safety amid change rather than trying to secure fixed ground.

chapters.title: Working with Anxiety
chapters.description: Matthew compares exposure therapy with mindfulness, then describes how attention can untangle thought, image, and body sensation when anxiety becomes overwhelming.

chapters.title: Honesty and Relinquishment
chapters.description: He closes by naming shame and the fear of being found out, then returns to the idea that care for the heart and relinquishment are the deeper forms of safety.

chapters.title: Closing Dedication
chapters.description: A brief closing sit and dedication of benefit ends the talk in a restrained, wishing tone.

title: Wholesome Doubt and Faith
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: What if doubt is not the obstacle, but the beginning of an honest practice? The talk traces a middle path between paralyzing skepticism and blind belief, using the Kalama Sutta, direct experience, and the difference between “belief” and “faith” to show how confidence can grow without closing down questioning. It then moves into the lived texture of practice: early unease, bright faith, steadier commitment, and the willingness to keep looking rather than settle for certainties. A later question-and-answer section turns to the historicity of the Buddha and the strain of right speech, bringing the teaching back to what can actually be tested in daily life.
short_summary: A quiet, practical talk on how wholesome doubt and faith work together, from the Kalama Sutta to everyday practice and speech.

chapters.title: Opening the Question
chapters.description: He introduces doubt and faith as intertwined qualities and frames the talk around wholesome doubt.

chapters.title: The Kalama Sutta
chapters.description: Matthew uses the Buddha’s guidance to the Kalama villagers as the main example of testing teachings by their effects.

chapters.title: Come See For Yourself
chapters.description: He reflects on the tension between being invited to find out for oneself and being guided by teachers.

chapters.title: Practice as Inquiry
chapters.description: Drawing on Sam Harris and his own language, he describes Buddhism as something to test in experience rather than accept as belief.

chapters.title: Doubt and First Steps
chapters.description: He turns to suffering, confidence, and the beginning of practice, including the way doubt can launch a search for freedom.

chapters.title: Bright Faith
chapters.description: Using Sharon Salzberg’s account and the idea of bright faith, he describes an energetic, hopeful phase that can propel commitment.

chapters.title: Letting Faith Mature
chapters.description: He speaks about a steadier faith that no longer needs to defend itself and remains open to revisiting what is actually known.

chapters.title: Questions and Right Speech
chapters.description: In the question period he addresses historical uncertainty about the Buddha, then responds to a listener struggling with right speech.

chapters.title: Mudita and Closing
chapters.description: A final question about sympathetic joy leads to a warning about skeptical counterexamples, then the talk closes with a short dedication.

title: How We Relate To Each Other
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: How do the insights of practice actually show up in the way we speak, listen, and meet one another? The talk begins with the holiday season and quickly turns to relationship as the place where meditation is tested, especially through wise speech, careful listening, and the gradual noticing of when speech is out of alignment with deeper intention. It then moves toward a quieter challenge: loosening rigid self-definition so we become less defensive, less easily offended, and more able to receive others fully. A final image of the empty boat brings the teaching into focus, suggesting that when self-clinging thins, reactivity has less to hold onto.
short_summary: The talk traces how Dharma practice enters relationship through wise speech, deep listening, and a loosening of self-clinging that makes us less defensive.

chapters.title: Holiday Relationship Theme
chapters.description: Matthew introduces the talk as a brief reflection on bringing practice into how we relate to each other during the holiday season.

chapters.title: Relational Beings
chapters.description: He describes human beings as fundamentally relational and says interpersonal life is where meditation insight is tested and integrated.

chapters.title: Practice in Relationship
chapters.description: He emphasizes that insights from the cushion need time and must take form in ordinary dealings if they are to matter.

chapters.title: Wise Speech
chapters.description: Matthew turns to speech as a direct field of practice, citing Joseph Goldstein and the simple standard of speech that is true, kind, and timely.

chapters.title: Trust and Feedback
chapters.description: He describes how careful speech creates trust and how mindfulness reveals when words are out of alignment with deeper intention.

chapters.title: Deep Listening
chapters.description: Matthew expands the practice to listening, including therapeutic listening, body awareness, and the way being heard can settle another person.

chapters.title: Letting Go of Ownership
chapters.description: He shifts to the teaching that nothing should be clung to as I or mine, connecting rigid self-definition with impaired love.

chapters.title: The Heavy Vase
chapters.description: Using the image of carrying a precious vase, he shows how self-protection narrows attention and makes us less open to others.

chapters.title: The Empty Boat
chapters.description: Matthew closes with Xuanzang's empty-boat teaching, suggesting that when the sense of self is emptied, anger and opposition ease.

title: Clinging & Letting Go
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: What if clinging is not just a problem to defeat, but a habit worth understanding for how much it seems to work? The talk moves from a simple claim about freedom as non-clinging, through poetry, personal examples, and Dharma teachings, into a careful look at the places clinging takes hold: sense pleasure, views, and self-view. It ends by turning toward the body, feeling, and the difficult work of staying open when letting go does not come easily.
short_summary: A grounded exploration of clinging and letting go, from the appeal of sense pleasure and being right to the practice of meeting feeling with equanimity.

chapters.title: Happiness and clinging
chapters.description: Brensilver begins by calling us philosophers of happiness and asking what we actually believe brings it.

chapters.title: Freedom as non-clinging
chapters.description: He uses Joseph Goldstein and Rumi to show that freedom points toward a mind not clinging to anything, while avoiding the idea that clinging is simply an enemy.

chapters.title: Respect the habit
chapters.description: Drawing on Gil Fronsdal, he argues for reverence rather than harshness toward our own clinging because it is powerful and persistent.

chapters.title: Ajahn Chah's simplicity
chapters.description: He turns to Ajahn Chah and Ajahn Amaro for a plain instruction—let go—and a reminder that knowing we should let go is already much of the path.

chapters.title: Craving and desire
chapters.description: Brensilver distinguishes craving from ordinary desire, and contrasts feverish grasping with the steadier wanting of wisdom.

chapters.title: Four fields of clinging
chapters.description: He moves through sense pleasure and views, using pizza and the appeal of being right to show how clinging hides in ordinary satisfactions and opinions.

chapters.title: Wrongness and self-view
chapters.description: A quote from Oscar Wilde leads into the claim that spiritual practice often means discovering we were wrong about ourselves, our problems, and even the Dharma.

chapters.title: Feeling and release
chapters.description: The talk ends with self-protective patterns, dependent origination, and the feeling-craving-clinging cycle, emphasizing embodied equanimity and the confidence that nothing inside need be broken by feeling.

chapters.title: Compassion and surrender
chapters.description: After describing moments when letting go seems impossible, he points to helplessness as a spur to compassion and closes with Rumi on wholehearted surrender.

title: Working with Anger: Part 3
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: How do you work with anger without turning it into self-blame, blame of others, or a rush to premature forgiveness? The talk moves from seeing anger as something conditioned and burdensome, to a careful definition of forgiveness that is not condoning, suppressing, or necessarily reconciling. It then turns toward a deeper practice: looking at how a fixed sense of self and a fixed image of others keep anger in place, and how clearer seeing can soften resentment into goodwill. Questions at the end bring the teaching into practical territory, including self-forgiveness, guilt versus remorse, and anger in response to injustice.
short_summary: A grounded talk on anger, forgiveness, and how clearer seeing can loosen resentment without denying harm.

chapters.title: Reviewing the ground
chapters.description: He briefly recaps the earlier teachings on accountability, moral charge, and the burden anger places on the heart.

chapters.title: Forgiveness and clear seeing
chapters.description: Matthew frames forgiveness as a maturing understanding of harm, using Shantideva, a Sufi saying, and Longfellow to widen perspective.

chapters.title: What forgiveness is not
chapters.description: He carefully distinguishes forgiveness from obligation, suppression, boundarylessness, and reconciliation.

chapters.title: Concepts feed anger
chapters.description: A story about an evangelical encounter shows how abstract ideas can intensify aversion until a real person is seen.

chapters.title: Self-clinging and threat
chapters.description: He turns to how rigid identity and shame create ego pressure, making anger a defense of the self.

chapters.title: Not self, not other
chapters.description: The talk shifts to the way we essentialize others, then uses Ajahn Sumedho's advice to 'not create them' as a practical correction.

chapters.title: Suffering and tenderness
chapters.description: He describes how intimate contact with suffering can open compassion, reduce alienation, and make harm feel less attractive.

chapters.title: A gift loosens resentment
chapters.description: Matthew introduces the Buddha's advice to give a gift, then tells a personal story of giving to someone he had resented.

chapters.title: Questions, remorse, and closing
chapters.description: In Q&A he clarifies pardon versus forgiveness, distinguishes guilt from remorse, and reflects on anger at injustice before closing with a wish for the practice to support less suffering.

title: Ripples of Happiness
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: How do we practice when the wish for our own well-being starts to feel too small? The talk moves from that ordinary starting point into a steadier intention to practice for others, with anger, generosity, and compassion as examples of how the work does not stay private. A social-network study on how happiness spreads through friends of friends then gives the teaching a concrete shape: whatever softens or frees the heart does not remain contained, but ripples outward.
short_summary: A brief talk on shifting from self-focused practice to the wider effects of practice, using a study of happiness spreading through social networks as support.

chapters.title: Opening the talk
chapters.description: Matthew briefly introduces the sit and sets up a few remarks before silence.

chapters.title: Intention in practice
chapters.description: He explains how practice may begin with personal well-being and later expand into a clear intention to practice for others.

chapters.title: Self and others
chapters.description: Matthew develops the idea that practice for oneself and practice for others are not separate, especially when anger, compassion, and generosity are involved.

chapters.title: A happiness study
chapters.description: He turns to a social-network study of about 5,000 people that examined how happiness moves through relationships.

chapters.title: Three degrees out
chapters.description: Matthew describes the study’s finding that happiness appears to spread across friends, friends of friends, and even farther out in the network.

chapters.title: What the study suggests
chapters.description: He quotes the researchers to underline that happiness is not only individual but a property of groups, then connects that to practice.

chapters.title: Dedicating the effort
chapters.description: The talk closes with an encouragement to dedicate practice to others when motivation fades and to find a posture that is sustainable.

title: Working with Anger: Part 2
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: How do you work with anger without turning it into self-blame, repression, or an endless story about other people? The talk moves from radical accountability to the claim that anger is sustained by repetition, then into a set of practices: noticing love, watching body sensations and inner talk, and investigating rather than blaming or despairing. It also brings in Ajahn Chah, Thich Nhat Hanh, Sharon Salzberg, Shantideva, and a few concrete ways of speaking honestly when anger has to be addressed with another person.
short_summary: A grounded talk on meeting anger with accountability, mindfulness, patience, and careful speech rather than blame or suppression.

chapters.title: Framing the work
chapters.description: Matthew sets up the talk as a practical exploration of anger, emphasizing accountability without self-blame and the hope that practice can change reactivity.

chapters.title: Ajahn Chah's warning
chapters.description: He tells a story about Ajahn Chah’s counterintuitive hatred meditation to show how exhausting anger is and how much it depends on rehearsal.

chapters.title: Hope and path
chapters.description: Matthew names anger as a burden while also stressing the heart's capacity to change, and widens the frame from technique to the larger Dharma path.

chapters.title: Investigate instead
chapters.description: Using the Buddha’s four responses to pain, he argues for investigation over blame or despair and points to the way anger hides delusion.

chapters.title: Care for anger
chapters.description: He quotes Thich Nhat Hanh on treating anger like a part of the body or a suffering child, and recommends not acting immediately when anger arises.

chapters.title: Mindfulness and vulnerability
chapters.description: Matthew describes the middle way between suppression and acting out, especially in close relationships, where honest anger can reveal vulnerability.

chapters.title: Speaking from the heart
chapters.description: He presents short phrases for difficult conversations and reflects on rupture, repair, and the slow work of meeting anger without defensiveness.

chapters.title: Body and urgency
chapters.description: Drawing on Goenka, he emphasizes body sensations, breath, and patience as ways to notice anger early and bear its pressure without reacting.

chapters.title: Questions and closing
chapters.description: In questions, he addresses anger in work settings and sustained conflict, then ends with a brief reflection on goodness and goodwill.

title: The Non-Paradox of Self and Not Self
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: If self is not something to defend, what is actually being protected? The talk begins by setting self-love and self-forgetting side by side, then moves through the worry that they must be in conflict, before arguing that kindness, cultivation, and insight into not-self can support one another. It stays practical rather than philosophical, using the Buddha’s silence on speculative questions, a psychologist’s caution about ego language, and a series of images about retreat, memory, and the mind’s “home base” to show how practice loosens identification and makes inner life feel safer and less burdened.
short_summary: A practical talk on self and not-self that argues self-kindness, mindfulness, and insight into emptiness can support one another rather than compete.

chapters.title: Two Path Tasks
chapters.description: He introduces the paired tasks of cultivating goodness and letting go of anything taken as I or mine.

chapters.title: Self-Clinging and Kindness
chapters.description: Matthew distinguishes harmful self-clinging from self-love and argues that kindness toward oneself supports insight.

chapters.title: Beyond Paradox
chapters.description: He explains why the subject feels paradoxical, then reframes self and not-self as experiences rather than metaphysical claims.

chapters.title: Buddha and Pragmatism
chapters.description: Using Vacchagotta’s question and Than Geoff’s comment, he sets aside speculative answers in favor of the end of suffering.

chapters.title: Ego and Development
chapters.description: A quote from Jack Engler helps separate psychological growth from spiritual language that turns ego into an enemy.

chapters.title: Mindfulness as Healing
chapters.description: He describes closing the eyes, meeting whatever arises, and letting practice digest memory, pain, and family history.

chapters.title: Suffering in the Self
chapters.description: Drawing on the Buddha and his own retreat stories, Matthew shows how self-clinging aims at safety, status, and feeling good.

chapters.title: Rumi and Sumedho
chapters.description: Rumi’s poem and Ajahn Sumedho’s line about personality and enlightenment shift the talk toward a freer stance.

chapters.title: Loving It to Death
chapters.description: He turns to subtle selfing, the sense of a home base of me, and closes with Norman Fisher on how practice quietly remakes a life.

title: Working with Anger: Part 1
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: What if anger is both natural and deeply misleading: a force that can sometimes signal injustice, yet so easily hardens into a story that feels absolutely true? The talk moves from listeners’ questions about outbursts, suppression, self-anger, and being around other people’s anger into a careful distinction between what happens and how we respond, and into the question of how to meet anger without rehearsing it. Drawing on Thich Nhat Hanh, Ajahn Chah, the Dalai Lama, Shantideva, and a Buddha image of anger’s “honeyed tip and poisoned root,” the speaker keeps returning to one practical point: notice the tangle, see the seduction, and take good care of the heat before it takes over.
short_summary: A grounded exploration of anger as natural, seductive, and workable, with attention to how to notice it, not rehearse it, and avoid turning it into identity.

chapters.title: Opening questions
chapters.description: Listener questions set up the talk around acted-out anger, suppression, and the experience of being around other people's anger.

chapters.title: Not rehearsing anger
chapters.description: Matthew introduces Thich Nhat Hanh's warning against rehearsing anger while still avoiding suppression.

chapters.title: Impact and response
chapters.description: He explores the gap between what happens to us and our reaction, and the search for a middle path between bottling anger and acting it out.

chapters.title: Responsibility without self-blame
chapters.description: The talk turns toward self-anger, the pain of greater mindfulness, and the reminder that we are responsible for our problems without being identical with them.

chapters.title: Not an angry person
chapters.description: Using Ajahn Chah, Matthew questions the identity story of being an angry person and places anger within the wider context of suffering and not-self.

chapters.title: Natural, but costly
chapters.description: He acknowledges that anger can sometimes be useful, especially around injustice, while also describing its limits and its role in human and historical suffering.

chapters.title: Shantideva and patience
chapters.description: A Shantideva passage on patience deepens the case that anger erodes trust, safety, and virtue, and Pema Chodron's reading softens the tone into practical self-admonition.

chapters.title: Seeing anger directly
chapters.description: Matthew describes anger's 'honeyed tip and poisoned root,' then explains how to untangle thought, feeling, and self-image in practice.

chapters.title: Closing reflection
chapters.description: He ends by inviting direct mindfulness of anger, followed by a short period of quiet and a closing blessing toward non-harming and peace.
